title: Mathematics + Financial Literacy: Simple Interest & Compound Interest
subject: Mathematics

questions.text: Riya invests ₹10,000 at 8% per annum for 3 years. What is the Simple Interest earned?
questions.explanation: SI = (P × R × T) / 100 = (10000 × 8 × 3) / 100 = ₹2,400. This is how a savings account works!
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.text: Aarav invests ₹5,000 at 10% per annum compounded annually for 2 years. What is the Compound Interest?
questions.explanation: A = P(1+r/100)^t = 5000(1.1)² = 6050. CI = 6050 - 5000 = ₹1,050. This is why compound interest is called 'the eighth wonder of the world'!
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.text: In a Systematic Investment Plan (SIP), Priya invests ₹1,000 monthly for 12 months earning 12% per annum. Roughly, how much more does she earn compared to a lump sum ₹12,000 at 12% for 6 months? (Use approximation)

options.text: SIP earns more due to rupee cost averaging

questions.explanation: SIP invests periodically, buying more units when prices are low (rupee cost averaging). For volatile markets, SIP typically gives better returns than lump sum over the same period.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.text: The 'Rule of 72' says an investment doubles in approximately 72 ÷ interest rate years. At 9% per annum, when does ₹20,000 become ₹40,000?
questions.explanation: 72 ÷ 9 = 8 years. This is a mental math shortcut used by financial advisors and investors worldwide!
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.text: A bank offers 6% simple interest and a mutual fund offers 6% compound interest (annually). After 5 years, which gives more returns on ₹1,00,000?
questions.explanation: SI after 5 years = ₹30,000. CI after 5 years = 1,00,000 × (1.06)⁵ - 1,00,000 ≈ ₹33,823. CI always beats SI at the same rate over time.
questions.subject: Mathematics
